D 2019 001 
EXECUTIVE ORDER 
Amending Executive Orders D 2013 026 and D 2018 007 to 
Provide Funding for Ongoing Emergency Response, Recovery, and Mitigation Efforts 
Connected with the 2013 Flooding and the Spring Creek Fire 
Pursuant to the authority vested in the Governor of the State of Colorado and, in 
particular, pursuant to Article IV, Section 2 of the Colorado Constitution and the relevant 
portions of the Colorado Disaster Emergency Act, C.R.S. § 24-33.5-701, et seq., I, Jared Polis, 
Governor of the State of Colorado, hereby issue this Executive Order amending Executive 
Orders D 2013 026, as amended, and D 2018 007 enabling state agencies to coordinate ongoing 
response, recovery, and mitigation efforts and making resources available for response, recovery, 
and mitigation activities connected with the 2013 Flooding and the Spring Creek Fire. 
I. 
Background and Purpose 
A. 
The Governor is responsible for meeting the dangers to the state and people 
presented by disasters. C.R.S. § 24-33.5-704(1). The Colorado Disaster 
Emergency Act defines a disaster as "the occurrence or imminent threat of 
widespread or severe damage, injury, or loss of life or property resulting from any 
natural cause or cause of human origin." C.R.S § 24-33.5-703(3). 
B. 
Due to historic flooding in Colorado in 2013 (2013 Flooding), Governor John 
Hickenlooper issued Executive Order D 2013 026, as amended by Executive 
Orders D 2013 027, D 2013 028, D 2013 030, D 2013 031, D 2013 033, D 2014 
001, D 2014 002, D 2014 006, D 2014 012, and D 2016 005 (collectively, 
Executive Order D 2013 026), declaring a disaster emergency in multiple 
counties. Executive Order D 2013-026 also provided for funding for ongoing 
response, recovery, and mitigation efforts in connection with the 2013 Flooding. 
C. 
The Spring Creek Fire started on June 27, 2018, in Costilla County, and spread 
into Huerfano County in June and July 2018. Accordingly, Governor John 
Hickenlooper issued Executive Order D 2018 007 declaring a disaster emergency 
and providing for funding for ongoing response, recovery, and mitigation efforts. 
D. 
The impacts of the 2013 Flooding and the Spring Creek Fire require additional 
resources for ongoing response, recovery, and mitigation efforts. 
136 State Capitol. Denver, CO 80203 I P 303.866.2471 I www.colorado.gov/governor 

Executive Order D 2019 001 
March 18, 2019 
Page 2 of 3 
E. 
This Executive Order amends Executive Order D 2013 026 and Executive Order 
D 2018 007 in order to provide for additional resources for ongoing response, 
recovery, and mitigation efforts connected with the 2013 Flooding and the Spring 
Creek Fire. Specifically, this Executive Order orders the following: 
i. 
Twelve million five hundred thousand dollars ($12,500,000) shall be 
transferred, as authorized by H.B. 18-1322, from the General Fund to the 
Disaster Emergency Fund to pay for response, recovery, and mitigation 
efforts connected with the 2013 Flooding; 
ii. 
Seven million dollars ($7,000,000) of unencumbered funds within the 
Disaster Emergency Fund shall be encumbered to pay for response, 
recovery, and mitigation efforts connected with the 2013 Flooding; 
iii. 
Seven million one hundred thousand dollars ($7,100,000) shall be 
transferred from the Controlled Maintenance Trust Fund to the Disaster 
Emergency Fund to pay for fire suppression and recovery efforts related to 
the Spring Creek Fire; and 
iv. 
Seven million nine hundred thousand dollars ($7,900,000) of 
unencumbered funds within the Disaster Emergency Fund shall be 
encumbered to pay for fire suppression and recovery efforts related to the 
Spring Creek Fire. 
II. 
Amendments 
A. 
Paragraph Il(C) of Executive Orders D 2013 026 and D 2013 027 and Paragraph 
Il(B) of Executive Orders D 2013 028, D 2013 031, D 2013 033, and D 2014 001 
are amended to read as follows: 
Pursuant to C.R.S. § 24-33.5-706(4), the funds in the Disaster Emergency Fund 
are found to be insufficient to pay for the flood response and recovery. Therefore, 
it is ordered that thirty eight million nine hundred fifty thousand dollars 
($38,950,000) be transferred into the Disaster Emergency Fund from the 
Controlled Maintenance Trust Fund established in C.R.S. § 24-75-302.5. 
Furthermore, an additional twelve million five hundred thousand dollars 
($12,500,000) is ordered to be transferred from the General Fund to the Disaster 
Emergency Fund, as authorized by H.B. 18-1322. 
It is further ordered that one hundred thirty one million dollars ($131,000,000) 
from the Disaster Emergency Fund is encumbered to pay for the flood response 
and recovery. The Director of the Office of Emergency Management is hereby 
authorized and directed to allocate the funding to the appropriate government 
agencies and non-profit organizations and execute awards, purchase orders, or 
other mechanisms to effect the allocation of the funds. The Director of the Office 

  
Executive Order D 2019 001 
March 18, 2019 
Page 3 of 3 
of Emergency Management is also authorized to allocate up to twenty million 
dollars ($20,000,000) to be used as short-term, no interest advances in order to 
provide any political subdivision of the state with short-term capital needed for 
flood response and recovery. These funds shall remain available for this purpose 
for sixty (60) months from December 9, 2019, and any unexpended funds shall 
remain in the Disaster Emergency Fund. 
B. 
Paragraph Il(C) of Executive Order D 2018 007 is amended as follows: 
Due to the insufficiency of funds in both the Emergency Fire Fund and the 
Disaster Emergency Fund established in C.R.S. § 24-33.5-706(2)(a) to pay for the 
state's share of fire suppression and recovery efforts related to the Spring Creek 
Fire, I order the transfer of twenty five million one hundred thousand dollars 
($25,100,000) from the Controlled Maintenance Trust Fund into the Disaster 
Emergency Fund pursuant to C.R.S. § 24-33.5-706(4)(6). I further encumber 
thirty three million dollars ($33,000,000) in the Disaster Emergency Fund to pay 
for the fire suppression, recovery, and mitigation efforts related to the Spring 
Creek Fire since June 27, 2018. These funds shall remain available for this 
purpose until sixty (60) months from the date of this Executive Order. 
III. 
Duration 
This Executive Order shall expire thirty (30) days from March 18, 2019, unless extended 
by further Executive Order, except that the funds described in Section II above shall remain 
available for the described purposes and the described time period stated in Section II. 
 GIVEN under my hand and
 Executive Seal of the State of
 Colorado this eighteenth day of  March, 2019.

D 2019 003 
EXECUTIVE ORDER 
Declaring a Disaster Emergency Due to Severe Winter Weather and Blizzard 
Conditions in Hinsdale, Elbert, Douglas, and El Paso Counties, and Authorizing 
Deployment of the Colorado National Guard 
Pursuant to the authority vested in the Governor of the State of Colorado and, in 
particular, pursuant to Article IV, Section 2 of the Colorado Constitution and the relevant 
portions of the Colorado Disaster Emergency Act, C.R.S. § 24-33.5-701, et seq., I, Jared Polis, 
Governor of the State of Colorado, hereby issue this Executive Order declaring a state of disaster 
emergency due to the severe winter weather and blizzard conditions in Hinsdale, Elbert, 
Douglas, and El Paso Counties ("Hinsdale, Elbert, Douglas, and El Paso Counties Severe Winter 
Weather and Blizzard Conditions"), enabling State agencies to coordinate response, recovery, 
and mitigation efforts, and making resources available to State agencies for response, recovery, 
and mitigation activities. 
Further, pursuant to the Colorado Constitution, Article IV, Section 5, and pursuant to 
C.R.S. § 28-3-104, I also hereby authorize deploying the Colorado National Guard for the
purpose of responding to the Hinsdale, Elbert, Douglas, and El Paso Counties Severe Winter
Weather and Blizzard Conditions.
I.
Background and Purpose
The Governor is responsible for meeting the dangers to the State and people presented 
by disasters. C.R.S. § 24-33.5-704(1). The Colorado Disaster Emergency Act defines a disaster 
as "the occurrence or imminent threat of widespread or severe damage, injury, or loss of life or 
property resulting from any natural cause or cause of human origin .... " C.R.S. § 24-33.5-
703(3). 
On March 13, 2019, an extremely powerful low pressure system developed over southern 
Colorado, likely setting a record for the lowest barometric pressure recorded in Colorado. The 
storm brought widespread rainfall to the plains followed by blizzard conditions over the 
northeast quadrant of the State, and dropped several feet of snow over the mountains. Blowing 
snow, low visibility, and hazardous travel continued into March 14, 2019. The National Weather 
Service reported wind gusts between 60 and 95 miles per hour. 
136 State Capitol, Denver. CO 80203 I P 303.866.2471 I www.colorado.gov/governor I 

Executive Order D 2019 003 
April3, 2019 
Page 2 of3 
The storm system stranded vehicles and people across the impacted areas, particularly in 
Elbert, Douglas, and El Paso counties. First responders were overwhelmed by the storm's 
impacts. 
In Hinsdale County, extreme avalanche danger forced several evacuations. An avalanche 
destroyed one home, owned by the Hinsdale County Sheriff, and injured three people. 
The State Emergency Operations Center supported and coordinated search and rescue 
activities and other requests from impacted jurisdictions. Division of Homeland Security and 
Emergency Management ("Division") Regional Field Managers supported local response efforts, 
and the Divisionts Public Information Officer was deployed to Hinsdale County to supplement 
public information needs. 
At approximately 2:30p.m. on March 13, 2019, I verbally declared a disaster emergency 
for Hinsdale, Elbert, Douglas, and El Paso Counties and activated the State of Colorado Hazard 
and Incident Response and Recovery Plan (the "Plan"). 
I further verbally authorized deployment and use of the Colorado National Guard for the 
purpose of responding to the Hinsdale, Elbert, Douglas, and El Paso Counties Severe Winter 
Weather and Blizzard Conditions. 
II. 
Declarations and Directives 
A. 
The Hinsdale, Elbert, Douglas, and El Paso Counties Severe Winter Weather and 
Blizzard Conditions and associated impacts constitute a disaster emergency under 
C.R.S § 24-33.5-701, et seq. My verbal order ofMarch 13, 2019, declaring a 
disaster emergency, is hereby memorialized by this Executive Order and shall 
have the full force and effect oflaw as if it were contained within this Executive 
Order. 
B. 
The Plan is hereby activated. All State departments and agencies shall take 
whatever actions may be required and requested by the director or acting director 
of the Office of Emergency Management pursuant to the Emergency Support 
Function 5 Annex of the Plan, including provision of appropriate staff and 
equipment as necessary. 
C. 
Pursuant to C.R.S. § 24-33.5-706(4), I find that the funds regularly appropriated 
to State and local agencies are insufficient to address this disaster emergency. 
Therefore, I order that two hundred thousand dollars ($200,000) from the State 
Disaster Emergency Fund is encumbered to pay for the State-level response, 
recovery, and mitigation efforts related to this disaster emergency. This amount is 
currently available in the Disaster Emergency Fund.

I. 
Background and Purpose 
The Governor is responsible for meeting the dangers to the State and people presented by

703(3).

created debris fields in drainages. The debris included soil. rocks. and trees. In April 2019, local

historic dams that arc both over I 00 years old. Both dams arc no longer in usc and have been

community governed by an I IOA. It is incumbent on the state to promote transparency and 
protect homeowners against practices that needlessly raise the cost of homeownership.

the Division of Real Estate to lead a stakeholder process that will complete a comprehensive

Declaring a Disaster Emergency Due to the Rockslide on State Highway 145 in Southwest 
Colorado 
Pursuant to the authority vested in the Governor of the State of Colorado and. in 
particular, pursuant to Article IV, Section 2 of the Colorado Constitution and the relevant 
portions ofthe Colorado Disaster Emergency Act. C.R.S. § 24-33.5-701, el seq., I. Jared Polis, 
Governor of the State of Colorado. hereby issue this Executive Order declaring a state of disaster 
emergency due to a rockslidc on State Highway 145 (SII 145) in Southwest Colorado on May 
24. 2019 (SH 145 Rockslide ). 
I. 
Background and Puroose 
The Governor is responsible for meeting the dangers to the State and people presented by 
disasters. C.R.S. § 24-33 .5-704( 1 ). The Colorado Disaster Emergency Act defines a disaster as 
"the occurrence or imminent threat of widespread or severe damage, injury, or loss of life or 
property resulting from any natural cause or cause of human origin ... .'' C.R.S. § 24-33.5-
703(3). 
On May 24,2019, two large rocks-one estimated to weigh 2.5 million pounds and the 
other estimated to weigh 8.5 million pounds-calved off an escarpment and fell approximately 
800 feet onto SH 145. One rock created an eight-foot deep trench across the entire width ofSH 
145. The other rock embedded in the roadway and blocked both lanes ofSH 145. 
The rocks destroyed a power pole adjacent to SH 145 and !ell the road impassable. The 
Colorado Department of Transportation (CDOT) closed SI-1 145 to all traffic. and it remained 
closed throughout the Memorial Day weekend while COOT crews blasted and removed the 
rocks. COOT's rockfall mitigation team conducted a site assessment and detennined that 
weather and freeze-thaw cycles may cause additional rockfall risk and mitigation is necessary. 
COOT opened one lane on May 26. 2019, to allow the travelling public through the area under 
controlled conditions. 
SH 145 is a primary transportation corridor in southwest Colorado. Two thousand 
vehicles, nine percent of which are trucks, use SH 145 each day. The route serves tourist traffic, 
which is critical to Dolores and San Miguel Counties' economies. SH 145 is also a key linkage 
for COOT's Bustang Outrider, a public transit service in southwest Colorado. CDOT has 
identified detour routes utilizing U.S. Highway I 60 via U.S. Highway 550; however, the detour 
adds at least three hours of travel time.

EXECUTIVE ORDER 
Amending Executive Order D 2019 005 to Extend the Declaration of Disaster Emergency 
Due to Avalanche Debris and Flooding Risk in Hinsdale County 
Pursuant to the authority vested in the Governor of the State of Colorado and, in 
particular, pursuant to Article IV, Section 2 of the Colorado Constitution and the relevant 
portions ofthe Colorado Disaster Emergency Act, C.R.S. § 24-33.5-701, et seq., I, Jared Polis, 
Governor of the State of Colorado, hereby issue this Executive Order amending Executive Order 
D 2019 005 to extend the declaration of a state of disaster emergency due to avalanche debris 
and flooding risk in Hinsdale County, enabling the continuance of State agency assistance to 
coordinate response, recovery, and mitigation efforts, and making resources available to State 
agencies for response, recovery, and mitigation activities. 
I. 
Background and Purpose 
The Governor is responsible for meeting the dangers to the State and people presented by 
disasters. C.R.S. § 24-33.5-704(1). The Colorado Disaster Emergency Act defines a disaster as 
"the occurrence or imminent threat of widespread or severe damage, injury, or loss oflife or 
property resulting from any natural cause or cause ofhuman origin .... " C.R.S. § 24-33.5-
703(3). 
In February and March 2019, Hinsdale County experienced numerous avalanches that 
created debris fields in drainages. The debris included soil, rocks, and trees. In April2019, local 
officials discovered several debris fields along and within Henson Creek, which contains two 
historic dams that are both over 1 00 years old. Both dams are no longer in use and have been 
partially breached to allow water to flow through them. However, local, state, and federal 
officials are concerned that the large volume of debris may plug the partial breaches in the two 
dams, causing them to hold water until they catastrophically fail. A catastrophic dam failure 
could result in floodwater reaching Lake City within ten to fifteen minutes. 
The Director of the Office of Emergency Management activated the State Emergency 
Operations Center to support and coordinate the efforts of state, local, and private entities in 
mitigating the risk of dam failure and preparing for response activities if mitigation efforts are 
insufficient. The Division of Homeland Security and Emergency Management Regional Field 
Manager is providing direct local support. 
At 9:34AM on May 6, 2019, I verbally declared a disaster emergency for the avalanche 
debris and flooding risk in Hinsdale County and activated the State of Colorado Hazard and 
Incident Response and Recovery Plan ("State Emergency Operations Plan"). I authorized State 
agency assistance and provided funding for response, recovery, and mitigation efforts in 
Executive Order D 2019 005.

Pursuant to the authority vested in the Governor of the State of Colorado and, in 
particular, Colorado Revised Statute§ 24-31- IOI(l)(a), I, Jared Polis, Governor of the State of 
Colorado, hereby issue this Executive Order designating Phil Weiser, Attorney General for the 
State of Colorado, as the State's prosecutor, to investigate and prosecute potential criminal 
activity involving the elected district attorney in Colorado's 13th Judicial District. 
I. 
Background and Pumosc 
The Governor is aware of allegations of misconduct and potential criminal 
activity involving the elected district attorney for the 13th Judicial District. The district attorney 
for the 14th Judicial District conducted a preliminary investigation into these allegations and 
concluded that further investigation is warranted. The conduct of elected officials is a matter of 
statewide importance, as is the integrity of the offices of the twenty-two elected district attorneys 
responsible for prosecuting crimes in our state. The Governor therefore finds that it is necessary 
for the Attorney General to act as the State's prosecutor and investigate and, where the evidence 
permits, prosecute potential criminal activity involving the elected district attorney for the 13th 
Judicial District. 
11. 
Directive 
I hereby appoint Phil Weiser, Attorney General for the State of Colorado, to be 
the State's prosecutor and direct him to take all necessary actions to investigate and prosecute, 
on behalf of the State of Colorado, all potential crimes relating to misconduct by 13th Judicial 
District Attorney.

In 2018, the Colorado Wildlife and Transportation Alliance (Alliance) was established to 
improve human safety while ensuring safe and successful migration of Colorado's big game 
wildlife each year. The Alliance includes CPW, Colorado Department of Transportation 
(COOT), Tribal Governments, federal agencies, and non-governmental partners that represent

record of success in Colorado. Coordination with government agencies, non-governmental 
organizations, and private landowners is critical to the safe migration of wildlife across 
numerous jurisdictions. Many heavily used migration routes were created through voluntary, 
proactive, and on-the-ground conservation measures.

opportunities for Colorado to further support big game wildlife migration and protect wildlife 
habitat. Recent technological advancements allow wildlife managers to better understand big 
game wildlife migration patterns. In addition, big game corridor work in Colorado has recently 
been bolstered by new funding opportunities from the United States Department of the Interior 
Secretarial Order 2018-3362. As such, it is timely for the State to conserve our migrating big 
game populations and their habitats. This Executive Order will ensure that future generations of 
Coloradans will enjoy a safe, prosperous relationship with the natural world and Colorado's 
native wildlife species.

EXECUTIVE ORDER 
Declaring a Disaster Emergency and Authorizing Employment of the Colorado National 
Guard to Provide Search and Rescue Support Due to the Shawnee Peak Fire 
Pursuant to the authority vested in the Governor of the State of Colorado and, in particular, 
pursuant to Article IV, Section 2 of the Colorado Constitution and the relevant portions of the 
Colorado Disaster Emergency Act, C.R.S. § 24-33.5-701, et seq., I, Jared Polis, Governor of the 
State of Colorado, hereby issue this Executive Order declaring a state of disaster emergency due 
to the Shawnee Peak Fire in Park County. 
Further, pursuant to Article IV, Section 5 of the Colorado Constitution and pursuant to 
C.R.S. § 28-3-104, I hereby authorize employing the Colorado National Guard for the purpose of 
providing standby personnel and aircraft for search and rescue support for firefighters responding 
to the Shawnee Peak Fire in Park County. 
I. 
Background and Purpose 
The Governor is responsible for meeting the dangers to the State and people presented by 
disasters. C.R.S. § 24-33.5· 704(1 ). The Colorado Disaster Emergency Act dcJincs a disaster as 
"the occurrence or imminent threat of widespread or severe damage, injury, or loss of life or 
property resulting from any natural cause or cause of human origin, including but not limited to 
fire." C.R.S. § 24-33.5-703(3). 
On August 26, 2019, the Shawnee Peak Fire was reported on federal land in Park County. 
The fire was burning in rugged terrain that was difficult or impossible to access using ground-
based vehicles. Firefighters accessed the area mostly on foot and worked in remote areas and 
hazardous conditions that limited evacuation and medical care for serious or critical injuries. 
The United States Forest Service has access to aircraft with the capability to extract injured 
personnel from rugged terrain; however, these aircraft were not immediately available. In the 
interim, federal authorities requested that alternate aircraft be placed on standby in the event that 
injured firefighters needed rescue. The Department of Public Safety coordinated with federal 
authorities to fulJill this request using the Colorado National Guard. 
The Colorado National Guard possesses aircraft with hoist capability for rescue missions. 
It was available to provide standby search and rescue support for any firefighters who were injured.

title: D 2019-014

I coLORADO 
I Governor Jared Polis 
D 2019 014 
EXECUTIVE ORDER 
Activating the Colorado National Guard to Assist with Election 
Cybcrsccurity Defense Efforts 
Pursuant to the authority vested in the Governor of the State of Colorado and, in 
particular, pursuant to Article IV, Section 5 ofthe Colorado Constitution and C.R.S. § 28-3-104, 
I, Jared Polis, Governor of the State of Colorado, hereby issue this Executive Order activating 
four (4) members of the Colorado National Guard Defensive Cyber Operations Element to assist 
with election cybersecurity defense efforts during the 2019 coordinated election. 
I. 
Background and Purpose 
Like many states, Colorado uses online voter registration systems that allow eligible 
citizens to access and update their voter registration information. These systems are protected by 
various security features that maintain confidentiality and prevent data tampering or interference 
with data. 
Even with such security features. online voter registration systems could provide an 
avenue for cyber actors to gain unlawful access to voter registration databases. Outside of 
Colorado, voter registration databases have been compromised by cyber actors hacking into 
various systems. While cyber actors are unable to modify voter records, breaches could result in 
the release of voters' personally identifiable information. The exposure of voters' personally 
identifiable information does not threaten the integrity of our State elections, but could 
undermine public confidence in the system and suppress voter registration. The Colorado 
National Guard Defensive Cyber Operations Element has unique cyber capabilities to assist in 
protecting critical election infrastructure. 
The Colorado Secretary of State has requested that the Colorado National Guard 
Defensive Cyber Operations Element provide election cybersecurity support during the 2019 
coordinated election. By activating four (4) members of the National Guard Defensive Cyber 
Operations Element, the National Guard will support the Secretary of State with election defense 
efforts up to and including the November 2019 election.

COLORADO 
Governor Jared Polis

EXECUTIVE ORDER 
Amending Executive Order D 2016 006 to Extend the Availability of Existing Funds 
for the Hayden Pass Fire in Fremont County 
Pursuant to the authority vested in the Governor of the State of Colorado and, in 
particular, pursuant to Article IV, Section 2 of the Colorado Constitution and the relevant 
portions ofthe Colorado Disaster Emergency Act, C.R.S. § 24-33.5-701, et seq., l, Dianne 
Primavera, Lieutenant Governor of the State of Colorado, hereby issue this Executive Order 
amending Executive Order D 2016 006 to extend the availability of existing funds for the 
Hayden Pass Fire in Fremont County (Hayden Pass Fire). 
I. 
Background and Purpose 
The Governor is responsible for meeting the dangers to the State and people presented 
by disasters. C.R.S. § 24-33.5-704(1). The Colorado Disaster Emergency Act defines a disaster 
as "the occurrence or imminent threat ofwidespread or severe damage, injury, or loss of life or 
property resulting from any natural cause or cause of human origin, including but not limited to 
fire .... " C.R.S. § 24-33.5-703(3). 
On July 8, 2016, lightning ignited a fire in the San Isabel National Forest. On July 10, 
2016, the fire increased in size and moved toward Coaldale in Fremont County. On July 12, 
2016, Fremont County declared a local emergency due to limited resources, the size ofthe fire, 
and lives and property at risk. Also on July 12, Lieutenant Governor Donna Lynne verbally 
declared a disaster emergency and authorized the use of the Colorado National Guard for 
assistance and resources related to the Hayden Pass Fire. 
On July 20, 2016, Governor John Hickenlooper issued. Executive Order D 2016 006, 
declaring a disaster emergency due to the Hayden Pass Fire and deploying the National Guard to 
assist with the fire response. Governor Hickenlooper transferred eight million dollars 
($8,000,000) from the Controlled Maintenance Trust Fund into the Disaster Emergency Fund, 
pursuant to C.R.S. § 24-33.5-706(4)(b), and encumbered the funds in the Disaster Emergency 
Fund to pay for fire suppression, response, and recovery efforts related to the Hayden Pass Fire. 
Governor Hickenlooper made the funds available for thirty-six (36) months from July 12, 2016. 
Due to the destructive nature of the Hayden Pass Fire and the complexities associated 
with affiliated recovery projects, a number of projects have not yet been fully completed. Local

title: D 2019-016
full_text:
D 2019 016 
EXECUTIVE ORDER 
Amending and Replacing Executive Order D 2018 026 Concerning the Greening of State 
Government 
Pursuant to the authority vested in the Governor of the State of Colorado and, in 
particular, pursuant to Article IV, Section 2 of the Colorado Constitution, I, Jared Polis, 
Governor of the State of Colorado, hereby issue this Executive Order amending and replacing 
Executive Orders D 2015 013 and D 2018 026 to reflect the State's new goals and directives to 
reflect the State's commitment to efficient and sustainable government operations. 
I. 
Background and Purpose 
The State of Colorado (State) is committed to providing Coloradans with exemplary State 
services. Those government operations need not come at the cost of our environment. Through a 
series of executive orders starting in 2003, Colorado has shown its commitment to making 
government operations more energy efficient and sustainable. This Executive Order builds on the 
State's prior greening government efforts, and establishes new goals and directives that will save 
taxpayer money and reduce the impact of State operations on our environment and public health. 
Since 2005, the Colorado Greening Government Coordinating Council (Council) has 
been at the forefront of these efforts. The Council has served as a resource to help agencies and 
departments managed by governor-appointed executives (referred to herein as Agencies and/or 
Departments or Agency and/or Department) reach sustainability goals. Executive Order D 2015 
013 renamed the Council to the Greening Government Leadership Council (GGLC), and 
required executive directors of Agencies and Departments to designate one representative to 
participate on the GGLC. 
The State's greening government efforts are off to a strong start. The 2018 Colorado 
GGLC Report Card found that the State achieved a: (1) 5% reduction in greenhouse gas 
emissions; (2) 2% reduction in energy use per square foot; and (3) 4.5% reduction in petroleum 
use per vehicle, over a fiscal year (FY) 2015 baseline. These results built on achievements 
outlined in the 2012 GGLC Report Card, in which the GGLC found that the State achieved a 
21% reduction in energy use per square foot in State facilities and a 5.3% reduction in petroleum 
use over a FY 2006-2007 baseline. But we need to do more to advance outstanding State 
government business practices that promote a healthy environment and foster economic and 
social vitality. 
136 State Capitol. Denver, CO 80203 I P 303.866.2471 I www.colorado.gov/governor 
I 

Executive Order D 2019 016 
December 16, 2019 
Page 2 of 8 
This Executive Order restates and amends, where applicable, Executive Order D 2018 
026 to continue and improve upon the critical work conducted under prior greening government 
orders. This Executive Order establishes new goals because the goals stated in D 2018 026 lapse 
in FY 2019-2020. In addition. this Executive Order restates and refines the greening government 
directives to ensure the State successfully meets the important goals in this Order. The goals and 
directives in this Executive Order supersede previous Greening Government Executive Order D 
2018 026. 
To accomplish these new goals and directives, the executive director of each Agency and 
Department shall support the development and implementation of plans, programs. and policies 
that incorporate sustainability practices into daily Agency and Department decision-making 
processes and long-term planning across all Agency and Department activities and functions. 
II. 
Greening Government Goals 
I hereby direct the GGLC to work with all Agencies and Departments to achieve the 
goals listed below. All Agencies and Departments shall: 
1. Greenhouse Gas Emissions 
a. Reduce greenhouse gas emissions by at least 10% below FY 2014-2015 
levels by the end of FY 2022-2023. Agencies and Departments shall meet 
this greenhouse gas reduction goal through improvements outlined in 
goals 2-4, below. 
2. Energy Management 
a. Reduce energy consumption per square foot by at least 15% by the end of 
FY 2022-2023 (normalized for weather), using FY 2014-2015 as a 
baseline. 
3. 
Renewable Energy 
a. Agencies and Departments will increase the percentage of renewable 
electricity consumed or purchased by State facilities to 5% by the end of 
FY 2022-2023. Such increase shall be in addition to the renewable energy 
provided by the utility as part of the overall power mix. 
b. Acceptable sources of renewable energy to be consumed or purchased to 
meet the renewable energy goals include: 
i. 
State-owned renewable energy systems; 

III. 
Executive Order D 2019 016 
December 16, 2019 
Page 3 of 8 
ii. 
Renewable energy purchased through a power purchase 
agreement; 
iii. 
Renewable energy procured through a solar garden subscription; 
iv. 
Utility renewable energy purchase programs; 
v. 
Other renewable energy installations developed in cooperation 
with a utility for which there is an agreement in place; and 
vi. 
Leased rooftop solar. 
4. Fleet Management 
Directives 
a. Agencies and Departments will reduce greenhouse gas emissions from 
State fleet vehicles by at least 15% by the end of FY 2022-2023, from a 
FY 2014-2015 baseline. 
b. Agencies and Departments will reduce greenhouse gas emissions by at 
least 7.5% by the end of FY 2022-2023 for vehicles categorized as special 
use. 
I hereby direct Agencies and Departments to undertake the following directives: 
1. Utility and Fleet Fuels Management. 
a. Agencies and Departments will track all utilities consumed in State 
facilities through a database determined and supported by the Colorado 
Energy Office (CEO). This database will document accurate utility use 
and cost information. CEO will administer the database and shall fund the 
development and maintenance of the database. CEO will help coordinate 
and identify support services to enable Agency and Department use of the 
database. 
b. Agencies and Departments will ensure accurate tracking and reporting of 
fleet fuel use on the Colorado Automotive Reporting System (CARS) as 
administered by State Fleet Management (SFM). The Colorado 
Department of Transportation (COOT) shall use approved reporting 
infrastructure for all vehicles that are not reported in CARS. 

2. Energy Efficiency and Renewable Energy. 
Executive Order D 2019 016 
December 16, 2019 
Page 4 of8 
a. Agencies and Departments shall include the following in their capital 
construction or capital renewal requests for new construction and 
substantial renovations: 
i. 
Analysis of how requested projects conform with the State's High 
Performance Certification Program; 
ii. 
Analysis of on-site renewable energy generation or renewable 
clean energy purchases; 
iii. 
Documentation of Agency and Department plans to meet 
requirements under C.R.S. § 24-30-1305.5 for reporting building 
utility performance data to the Office of the State Architect (OSA) 
through either a nationally recognized building certification 
program, a CEO approved utility tracking database, or another 
OSA accepted procedure; 
iv. 
Provide documentation or plans showing that at least 20% of 
parking spaces will be pre-wired for charging, and that at least 5% 
will have EV chargers installed; and 
v. 
Provide documentation that the Agency or Department explored 
options for the electrification of building systems, and such review 
shall include a full life cycle cost analysis of the impact of 
electrification. 
b. CEO and the Department of Personnel & Administration (DPA) shall 
consult with the Governor's Office of State Planning and Budgeting 
(OSPB) to develop model contracts and expedite procurement of 
renewable energy through: 
i. 
Agency and Department-owned renewable energy installations; 
ii. 
Power purchase agreements; and 
iii. 
Utility solar gardens. 
c. As part of any renewable energy procurement, Agencies and Departments 
will explore whether the installation of energy storage systems is feasible, 
and if cost effective, any renewable energy installation shall include 
energy storage. 

Executive Order D 20 I 9 016 
December 16, 2019 
Page 5 of 8 
d. Agencies and Departments will explore the possibility of utilizing unused 
budget authority in utility line items to complete energy efficiency and 
renewable energy projects in subsequent years. 
e. Agencies and Departments identified as good candidates for 
comprehensive Energy Perfonnance Contracting (EPC) through EPC 
feasibility studies shall work with CEO and OSA to implement energy 
efficiency measures through EPC, including LED lighting retrofit projects. 
f. Agencies and Departments that are not good candidates for a 
comprehensive EPC through EPC feasibility studies shall pursue 
comprehensive LED lighting retrofit projects for State facilities using 
either EPC or other funding, to be completed by June 30, 2022 unless the 
use case indicates that LEDs are not practicable and a waiver is granted by 
the Agency or Department's executive director and approved by the 
executive directors of CEO and DPA. 
g. For controlled maintenance and capital renewal budget requests, Agencies 
and Departments shall make best efforts to include EPC wherever 
possible. Where it is not possible, Agencies and Departments shall explain 
why including EPC is not suitable and why additional funding cannot be 
secured to support the project. Institutes of Higher Education are 
encouraged to comply with this directive. OSPB will take this infonnation 
into consideration while developing the Governor's budget. 
3. State Fleet Vehicle Fuel Efficiency and Zero Emission Fleet Vehicles. 
a. This Executive Order covers all Agency and Department State fleet 
vehicles, including those managed by DPA and COOT. Institutions of 
Higher Education are encouraged to comply with this Executive Order's 
directives for emissions reduction. 
b. Members of the CEO,  CDOT, Colorado Department of Public Health and 
Environment (CDPHE) and DPA shall establish a State Fleet Sub-Council 
to help develop, implement, and improve programs, plans, and policies 
that save money, reduce emissions, promote domestic fuel use, and 
conserve natural resources. The Sub-Council, led by DPA, shall include 
fleet coordinators or other agency representatives and members from 
institutions of higher education as appropriate. Sub-Council members shall 
be appointed by agency and department Executive Directors. The Sub­
Council shall: 

Executive Order D 2019 016 
December 16, 2019 
Page 6 of 8 
i. 
Develop standard procedures and formulas for modeling and 
monitoring potential alternative fuel vehicles (AFVs) and fuel 
reduction efforts that link acquisition and operations budgets; 
ii. 
Identify and evaluate other fuel-saving practices; and 
iii. 
Evaluate alternative financing options for State fleet vehicles 
including leasing, energy performance contracting, and other 
options that may reduce costs for the State vehicles. 
c. Agencies and Departments shall create a vehicle management team 
(VMT) that includes, at a minimum, the: (1) fleet coordinator or manager; 
(2) budget director; and (3) greening government representative. VMTs 
shall pursue implementation of a telematics system for use on all State 
fleet vehicles. Agencies and Departments shall support the installation of 
telematics on all Agency and Department vehicles and utilize the 
telematics systems with VMTs for the deployment of future alternative 
fuel vehicle technologies. 
d. CEO and DPA, with the participation and support of all Agencies and 
Departments, shall undertake a facility assessment for EV charging 
infrastructure at State-owned facilities. Using this information, funding 
requests shall be submitted to OSPB for consideration for the FY 2021-
2022 budget in order to install equipment. 
e. Agencies and Departments will prioritize purchase of EVs for light-duty 
applications. In cases where a plug-in hybrid electric vehicle (PHEV) or a 
battery electric vehicle (BEV) is determined to be a suitable replacement, 
Agencies and Departments will work with SFM to procure such vehicles. 
Agencies must provide written justification for any light duty vehicle 
purchases that are nor BEV or PHEV. Such justification shall be reviewed 
by the Agency or Department's executive director, SFM, and CEO before 
the vehicle may be purchased. SFM will make the final approval in 
collaboration with the Governor's Office. 
f. Prioritizing the purchase of EVs will increase the need for charging 
infrastructure prior to the implementation of an enterprise solution. Until 
such time that an enterprise solution is fully analyzed and associated 
funding requests are considered, Agencies and Departments will continue 
to install EV charging infrastructure to support EVs added to the Agency 
or Department's fleet. All new EV charging infrastructure will be reported 
to the CEO to be included in the final analysis for an enterprise solution. 

Executive Order D 2019 016 
December 16, 2019 
Page 7 of 8 
g. CEO and DPA shall work with OSPB and the State controller to develop a 
policy for take-home EVs that allows for the reimbursement of home 
charging costs for State employees when operational use requires it. 
h. DPA shall work with Agencies and Departments to place electric or other 
zero emission mid and heavy-duty vehicles in those cases where this type 
of vehicle meets an Agency or Department's programmatic needs. 
i. Each EV that is added to the fleet shall be used as an opportunity to 
provide education and outreach to the public and State employees. This 
shall include vehicle wraps, brochures, and other information within the 
vehicle, as well as training for vehicle users. 
j. All Agencies and Departments shall evaluate opportunities to improve 
commuting options for employees to reduce their carbon footprint, 
including workplace charging for electric vehicles and telecommuting. 
k. DPA and  CDOT shall establish policies and procedures to promote the 
cost-effective use of non-petroleum fuel vehicles and other fleet efficiency 
improvements wherever feasible. 
4. 
Agency and Department Staffing and Training Directives. 
a. Agencies and Departments shall work with OSPB and DPA to identify 
resources and staffing necessary to focus on energy efficiency, energy 
conservation, and renewable energy effort goals. DPA will work with 
Agencies and Departments to determine an appropriate title and position 
description for any staff. 
b. All Agencies and Departments with fleet vehicles shall work with DPA 
and OSPB to identify resources and staffing necessary for the management 
of an Agency or Department's fleet, including executing on EV 
opportunities. 
5. Leased Facilities. 
a. If an Agency or Department leases space that is not owned by the State, 
the leased space is exempt from this Executive Order's goals unless the 
lease meets all of the following criteria: 
i. 
The lease is for 75% or more of the building's rentable square feet; 
ii. 
The leased space's utilities are submetered; and 

Executive Order D 2019 016 
December 16, 20 I 9 
Page 8 of 8 
iii. 
The rentable square feet of the leased area is 10,000 square feet or 
more. 
b. Leased space must convert to LED lighting by June 30, 2022 unless a 
waiver is granted by the director of DPA. 
c. OSA's Real Estate Program shall update the Green Lease Policy and 
Green Lease Scoring Matrix to reflect the directives of this Executive 
Order. 
d. OSA's Real Estate and Energy Management Programs shall review and 
revise the Green Lease Policy and Green Lease Scoring Matrix in 
accordance with this Executive Order and shall ensure that EV charging 
infrastructure is included as a scoring criteria for all leased space when 
applicable. 
e. 
In accordance with this Executive Order and the revised Green Lease 
Policy and Green Lease Scoring Matrix, OSA's Real Estate Program shall 
review all existing lease agreements to detennine whether the leased space 
is exempt from this Executive Order's goals and directives. 
f. Agencies and Departments need not tenninate noncompliant leases but 
shall make a good faith effort to bring any noncompliant leases into 
compliance with this Executive Order. 
6. Agencies and Departments shall implement these directives within existing 
budgets and authorities. If necessary, Agencies and Departments shall engage 
with OSPB to identify resource requirements and incorporate those requirements 
into the annual budget development process for any actions to be implemented in 
future fiscal years. 
7. Agencies and Departments, with the support of CEO, shall participate in local 
benchmarking and transparency ordinances by making utility data and other 
required infonnation available for State-owned buildings located in those 
jurisdictions within existing resources. 
8. By December 15 of each year, the GGLC shall prepare a report card for the 
administration that highlights progress toward goals and Agency and Department 
accomplishments for the prior fiscal year. All Agencies and Departments shall 
provide the GGLC with any necessary infonnation to complete the report card. 

  
IV. 
Duration 
Executive Order D 2019 016 
December 16, 2019 
Page 9 of8 
Executive Orders D 2015 013 and D 2018 026, as well as all previous Executive Orders 
relating to Greening Government, are hereby amended, where applicable, by this Executive 
Order. This Executive Order shall remain in effect unless modified or rescinded by future 
Executive Order of the Governor. 
 GIVEN under my hand and the
 Executive Seal of the State of
 Colorado, this sixteenth day of December, 2019.  
Gs

COLORADO 
Governor Jared Polis 
D 2019 017 
EXECUTIVE ORDER 
Proclamation 
Declaration of Vote on Proposition CC- Retain Revenue for Education and Transportation 
and Proposition DD- Authorize and Tax Sports Betting 
Pursuant to the authority vested in the Governor ofthe State of Colorado and, in 
particular, pursuant to Article v. Section 1(4)(a) of the Colorado Constitution, I, Jared Polis, 
Governor of the State of Colorado, hereby issue this proclamation declaring the vote on 
Proposition CC and Proposition DO. 
I. 
Background and Purpose 
Proposition CC and Proposition DO were initiated by the people of the state at the 
biennial regular election held on November 5, 2019. Secretary of State Jena Griswold certified 
on December 2, 2019 that she had canvassed the "Abstract of Votes Cast" and that the measure 
was approved by a majority of the votes cast. 
Pursuant to Article V, Section 1(4)(a) of the Colorado Constitution, measures initiated by 
or referred to the people of the state shall become law or part of the constitution when approved 
by a majority of the votes cast, and shall take effect from and after the date of the official 
declaration of the vote by proclamation of the governor, but not later than thirty days after the 
vote has been canvassed. 
II. 
Declaration 
Accordingly, I hereby issue this proclamation declaring the vote on the following 
measures initiated by the people on November 5, 2019: 
Proposition CC 
Without raising taxes and to better fund public schools, higher education, and roads, 
bridges, and transit, within a balanced budget, may the state keep and spend all the 
revenue it annually collects after June, 30, 2019, but is not currently allowed to keep 
and spend under Colorado law, with an annual independent audit to show how the 
retained revenues are spent?

title: D 2019-018
full_text:
D 2019 018 
EXECUTIVE ORDER 
Declaring a Disaster Emergency Due to Severe Winter Weather and Blizzard 
Conditions in Northeast Colorado, and Authorizing Deployment of the Colorado National 
Guard 
Pursuant to the authority vested in the Governor of the State ofColorado and, in 
particular, pursuant to Article IV, Section 2 of the Colorado Constitution and the relevant 
portions of the Colorado Disaster Emergency Act, C.R.S. § 24-33.5-701, et seq., I, Jared Polis, 
Governor of the State of Colorado, hereby issue this Executive Order declaring a state ofdisaster 
emergen~y due to the severe winter weather and blizzard conditions in Larimer, Weld, Logan, 
Sedgwick, Phillips, Boulder, Morgan, Washington, Yuma, Clear Creek, Adams, Broomfield, 
Jefferson, Denver, Arapahoe, Douglas, Elbert, Lincoln and Kit Carson counties ("Northeast 
Colorado Counties Severe Winter Weather and Blizzard Conditions"), enabling State agencies to 
coordinate response efforts, and making resources available to State agencies for response 
activities. 
Further, pursuant to the Colorado Constitution, Article IV, Section 5, and pursuant to 
C.R.S. § 28-3-104, I also hereby authorize employing the Colorado National Guard for the 
purpose of responding to the Northeast Colorado Counties Severe Winter Weather and Blizzard 
Conditions. 
I. 
Background and Purpose 
The Governor is responsible for meeting the dangers to the State and people presented 
by disasters. C.R.S. § 24-33.5-704(1). The Colorado Disaster Emergency Act defines a disaster 
as "the occurrence or imminent threat of widespread or severe damage, injury, or loss of life or 
property resulting from any natural cause or cause of human origin ...." C.R.S. § 24-33.5­
703(3). 
On November 25, 2019, meteorologists predicted that an extremely powerful winter 
storm would affect Colorado-particularly counties along and north of Interstate 70 and east of 
Interstate 25. The National Weather Service issued a blizzard warning for a large portion of the 
highly-affected areas. Based on experience with similar storms in March and April 2019, the 
State expected that stranded travelers on roadways would require sheltering. The Colorado 
National Guard is the most effective available resource to support search and rescue operations 
in these conditions. 
136 State Capitol. Denver, CO 80203 IP 303.866.2471 Iwww.colorado.gov/governor ! 

Executive Order D 2019 018 
December 20, 2019 
Page 2 of3 
By November 26, 2019, some areas ofthe State received over 30 inches of snow and 
experienced wind gusts up to 40 miles per hour that created blowing and drifting snow. This 
storm forced closures ofmajor highways and cancellation of passenger flights. The Director of 
the Office ofEmergency Management activated the State Emergency Operations Center to 
support and coordinate search and rescue activities and other requests from impacted 
jurisdictions. The Division ofHomeland Security and Emergency Management Regional Field 
Managers and Division of Fire Prevention and Control Snow Taskforce supported local response 
efforts. 
At 5:24 p.m. on November 25, 2019, I verbally declared a disaster emergency for the 
Northeast Colorado Counties Severe Winter Weather and Blizzard Conditions and activated the 
State ofColorado Emergency Operations Plan. At the same time, I verbally authorized 
deployment and use ofthe Colorado National Guard for the purpose ofsearch and rescue 
operations resulting from the Northeast Colorado Counties Severe Winter Weather and Blizzard 
Conditions. 
II. 
Declarations and Directives 
A. 
The Northeast Colorado Counties Severe Winter Weather and Blizzard 
Conditions and associated impacts constitute a disaster emergency under C.R.S § 
24-33.5-701, et seq. My verbal order ofNovember 25, 2019, declaring a disaster 
emergency is hereby memorialized by this Executive Order and shall have the full 
force and effect of law as if it were contained within this Executive Order. 
B. 
Pursuant to C.R.S. § 28-3-104, my verbal orders ofNovember 25, 2019, 
authorizing deployment and use of the National Guard for search and rescue 
operations associated with the Northeast Colorado Counties Severe Winter 
Weather and Blizzard Conditions are hereby memorialized by this Executive 
Order and shall have the full force and effect of law as ifthey were contained 
within this Executive Order. 
C. 
The State Emergency Operations Plan is hereby activated. All State departments 
and agencies shall take whatever actions required and requested by the Director or 
Acting Director of the Office of Emergency Management pursuant to the 
Emergency Support Function 5 Annex of the State Emergency Operations Plan, 
including provision ofappropriate staff and equipment as necessary. 
D. 
Pursuant to C.R.S. § 24-33.5-706(4), the funds regularly appropriated to State and 
local agencies are insufficient to address this disaster emergency. Therefore, I 
order twenty-one thousand dollars ($21,000) from the Disaster Emergency Fund 

  
Executive Order D 2019 018 
December 20, 2019 
Page3of3 
to be encumbered to pay for response efforts related to this disaster emergency. 
This amount is currently available in the Disaster Emergency Fund. 
E.
Pursuant to C.R.S. § 24-33.5-705.4(5), the Director of the Office of Emergency
Management is hereby authorized and directed to allocate the funding to the
appropriate government agencies and non-profit organizations and execute
awards, purchase orders, or other mechanisms to allocate the funds. These funds
shall remain available for this purpose until six (6) months from the date of this
Executive Order and any unexpended funds shall remain in the Disaster
Emergency Fund.
lll.
Duration
This Executive Order shall expire thirty (30) days from November 25, 2019, unless
extended further by Executive Order, except that the funds described in Section ll(C) above shall 
remain available for the described purposes for six (6) months from the date of this Executive 
Order. 
GIVEN under my hand and the 
Executive Seal of the State of 
Colorado, this twentieth day of 
December, 2019. 
Jared Polis 
Governor
